Outcomes.Text: Explain the structure of matplotlib's pyplot interface and how it manages figures and axes.
Apply pyplot functions to create line plots, scatter plots, bar charts, and histograms from engineering data.
Demonstrate the use of subplots to organize multiple visualizations within a single figure.
Create publication-quality figures by customizing labels, titles, legends, colors, and styles.
Identify the differences between pandas Series and DataFrame structures and when to use each.
Analyze engineering datasets using pandas operations including reading, filtering, sorting, and descriptive statistics.

Topics.Title: Introduction to Matplotlib and the pyplot Interface
Topics.Summary: Introduces the matplotlib library and its pyplot module, explaining how the interface manages the figure and axes objects that underlie every plot.

Elements.Title: What is Matplotlib?
Elements.BodyText: Matplotlib is a comprehensive Python library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations. It is the foundational plotting library in the scientific Python ecosystem.
Elements.Notes: Matplotlib was originally modeled after MATLAB's plotting syntax, making it approachable for engineers transitioning from that environment.

Items.Text: Matplotlib integrates seamlessly with NumPy arrays and pandas DataFrames.
It supports a wide range of output formats including PNG, PDF, and SVG.
The library is highly customizable, allowing fine-grained control over every visual element.

Elements.Title: The pyplot Module
Elements.BodyText: The pyplot module provides a state-based interface to matplotlib, allowing users to create and modify figures with simple function calls. It is typically imported as plt.
Elements.Notes: The conventional import statement is: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt. This alias is used universally in documentation and community code.

Items.Text: pyplot tracks the 'current figure' and 'current axes' automatically, reducing boilerplate code.
Functions such as plt.plot(), plt.show(), and plt.savefig() operate on the current active figure.
For more complex layouts, pyplot can be combined with the object-oriented matplotlib API.

Elements.Title: Figures and Axes Objects
Elements.BodyText: In matplotlib, a Figure is the top-level container for all plot elements, while an Axes is the region within the figure where data is plotted. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for building complex visualizations.
Elements.Notes: A single Figure can contain multiple Axes objects, which is the basis for creating subplot layouts.

Items.Text: plt.figure() creates a new Figure object and makes it the current figure.
Each Axes object has its own x-axis, y-axis, title, and data space.
Accessing Axes objects directly enables more precise control over individual plot panels.

Elements.Title: Displaying and Saving Figures
Elements.BodyText: Once a plot is constructed, it can be rendered to the screen with plt.show() or saved to a file with plt.savefig(). Proper use of these functions ensures figures appear and are preserved correctly.
Elements.Notes: In Jupyter notebooks, plt.show() is often unnecessary because figures render inline automatically with the %matplotlib inline magic command.

Items.Text: plt.savefig('filename.png', dpi=300) saves a high-resolution raster image.
plt.show() should typically be called once per figure after all plotting commands.
plt.close() releases memory associated with a figure after it is no longer needed.

Topics.Title: Line Plots and Scatter Plots
Topics.Summary: Covers the creation and customization of line plots and scatter plots, the two most common chart types for visualizing continuous and relational engineering data.

Elements.Title: Creating a Basic Line Plot
Elements.BodyText: A line plot is created using plt.plot(x, y), which connects data points with line segments. It is ideal for showing trends over a continuous variable such as time or distance.
Elements.Notes: If only one array is passed to plt.plot(), it is treated as the y-values and the index is used as x.

Items.Text: plt.plot() accepts NumPy arrays, Python lists, or pandas Series as input.
Multiple lines can be added to the same axes by calling plt.plot() repeatedly.
Line style, color, and marker can be specified in a single format string (e.g., 'r--o').

Elements.Title: Customizing Line Plots
Elements.BodyText: Line plots can be customized with keyword arguments to control color, line width, style, and markers. Meaningful labels and titles improve interpretability.
Elements.Notes: Common keyword arguments include color, linewidth (or lw), linestyle (or ls), and marker. For example: plt.plot(x, y, color='blue', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', marker='o').

Items.Text: Use plt.xlabel() and plt.ylabel() to add descriptive axis labels.
plt.title() adds a title to the current axes.
plt.legend() renders a legend when plots include a label keyword argument.

Elements.Title: Creating a Scatter Plot
Elements.BodyText: A scatter plot is created with plt.scatter(x, y) and displays individual data points without connecting lines. It is useful for exploring correlations and distributions between two variables.
Elements.Notes: plt.scatter() offers more per-point customization than plt.plot() with markers, allowing each point to have a different color or size encoded by a third variable.

Items.Text: The s parameter controls marker size and can accept an array to vary size per point.
The c parameter maps a third variable to point color when combined with a colormap.
plt.colorbar() adds a color scale legend when a continuous variable is mapped to color.

Elements.Title: Axis Limits and Scaling
Elements.BodyText: Controlling the visible range of axes ensures that important data features are not clipped or obscured. Matplotlib provides functions to set both linear and logarithmic scales.
Elements.Notes: Logarithmic scales are particularly useful in engineering for visualizing data that spans several orders of magnitude, such as frequency response plots.

Items.Text: plt.xlim(min, max) and plt.ylim(min, max) set explicit axis boundaries.
plt.xscale('log') and plt.yscale('log') switch axes to logarithmic scale.
plt.axis('equal') enforces equal aspect ratio, important for geometric or spatial data.

Topics.Title: Bar Charts and Histograms
Topics.Summary: Explains how to create bar charts for categorical comparisons and histograms for examining the distribution of numerical data in engineering contexts.

Elements.Title: Creating a Bar Chart
Elements.BodyText: Bar charts are created with plt.bar(categories, values) and are used to compare discrete groups or categories. They are commonly used to display experimental results across different conditions.
Elements.Notes: Horizontal bar charts can be created with plt.barh(), which may be preferable when category labels are long.

Items.Text: The width parameter adjusts bar width, and color accepts a list for per-bar coloring.
Error bars can be added using the yerr parameter to represent uncertainty.
Grouped bar charts are created by offsetting multiple bar calls with adjusted x positions.

Elements.Title: Annotating Bar Charts
Elements.BodyText: Adding value labels or annotations to bar charts makes them easier to interpret, especially in technical reports. matplotlib provides text placement functions for this purpose.
Elements.Notes: plt.text(x, y, str(value)) places a string at specified data coordinates. Aligning text to bar tops requires computing the correct y offset from bar heights.

Items.Text: plt.annotate() supports advanced annotation with arrows and connection styles.
Iterating over bar container objects allows programmatic placement of value labels.
Font size and alignment can be controlled with fontsize and ha keyword arguments.

Elements.Title: Creating a Histogram
Elements.BodyText: Histograms are created with plt.hist(data, bins=n) and display the frequency distribution of a single continuous variable. They are essential for understanding data spread and identifying outliers in engineering measurements.
Elements.Notes: The bins parameter can accept an integer for automatic bin sizing or a list of explicit bin edges. Choosing an appropriate bin count is important to avoid under- or over-smoothing the distribution.

Items.Text: density=True normalizes the histogram so that the total area equals one, producing a probability density.
The edgecolor parameter draws borders around bars, improving visual clarity.
Overlapping histograms benefit from the alpha parameter to control transparency.

Elements.Title: Comparing Distributions with Histograms
Elements.BodyText: Multiple datasets can be compared by overlaying histograms on the same axes. Proper use of transparency and color enables effective visual comparison of distributions.
Elements.Notes: When comparing two distributions, setting alpha=0.5 for both histograms allows overlap regions to be visible. Adding a legend with distinct labels is essential for disambiguation.

Items.Text: Calling plt.hist() twice on the same axes overlays both distributions automatically.
plt.legend() should be used to clearly identify each distribution.
Cumulative histograms (cumulative=True) are useful for showing percentile thresholds.

Topics.Title: Subplots and Figure Layout
Topics.Summary: Demonstrates how to create multi-panel figures using subplots, enabling side-by-side comparison of different plots within a single publication-ready figure.

Elements.Title: Creating Subplots with plt.subplots()
Elements.BodyText: The plt.subplots(nrows, ncols) function creates a Figure and a grid of Axes objects in one call. It is the recommended approach for building multi-panel layouts.
Elements.Notes: plt.subplots(2, 2) returns a 2x2 array of Axes. Each subplot is accessed by indexing, e.g., axs[0, 1] for the top-right panel.

Items.Text: The returned fig and axs objects give direct access to the figure and each axes panel.
sharex=True and sharey=True link axis ranges across subplots for easier comparison.
figsize=(width, height) passed to plt.subplots() controls the overall figure dimensions in inches.

Elements.Title: Plotting on Individual Axes
Elements.BodyText: When using subplots, plotting commands are called on individual Axes objects rather than on plt directly. This object-oriented approach provides precise control over each panel.
Elements.Notes: Most pyplot functions have equivalent Axes methods: plt.plot() corresponds to ax.plot(), plt.set_xlabel() corresponds to ax.set_xlabel(), and so on.

Items.Text: ax.plot(), ax.scatter(), ax.bar(), and ax.hist() work identically to their pyplot counterparts.
ax.set_title(), ax.set_xlabel(), and ax.set_ylabel() set per-panel labels and titles.
ax.legend() adds a legend scoped to that individual subplot panel.

Elements.Title: Adjusting Layout and Spacing
Elements.BodyText: Subplot panels can overlap or clip labels if spacing is not adjusted. matplotlib provides tools to automatically or manually correct figure layout.
Elements.Notes: plt.tight_layout() is the quickest fix for overlapping labels and should be called just before plt.show() or plt.savefig().

Items.Text: plt.tight_layout() automatically adjusts subplot parameters to prevent overlap.
plt.subplots_adjust(hspace, wspace) provides manual control over vertical and horizontal spacing.
fig.suptitle() adds a centered title above all subplots in the figure.

Elements.Title: Using GridSpec for Complex Layouts
Elements.BodyText: For non-uniform subplot grids, matplotlib.gridspec.GridSpec allows plots of different sizes to share a figure. This is useful for dashboard-style engineering figures.
Elements.Notes: GridSpec can create layouts where one plot spans multiple rows or columns, for example a wide summary plot above a row of detail panels.

Items.Text: GridSpec is imported from matplotlib.gridspec and passed a row and column count.
Subplot areas are defined by slicing the GridSpec object, e.g., gs[0, :] for a full-width top panel.
fig.add_subplot(gs[row, col]) creates an Axes object in the specified grid cell.

Topics.Title: Publication-Quality Figure Styling
Topics.Summary: Covers techniques for elevating plot aesthetics to meet publication and report standards, including style sheets, custom colors, annotations, and saving high-resolution outputs.

Elements.Title: Using Style Sheets
Elements.BodyText: Matplotlib includes built-in style sheets that instantly apply consistent aesthetics to all figures in a script. Selecting an appropriate style reduces manual customization effort.
Elements.Notes: plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid') applies a clean grid style popular in scientific publications. A list of available styles is returned by plt.style.available.

Items.Text: plt.style.use('style_name') applies a style globally for the duration of the script.
Styles can be combined by passing a list: plt.style.use(['style1', 'style2']).
Context managers (with plt.style.context('style'):) apply a style temporarily without changing global settings.

Elements.Title: Customizing Colors and Colormaps
Elements.BodyText: Color choices affect both the aesthetic quality and accessibility of a figure. Matplotlib supports named colors, hex codes, RGB tuples, and scientific colormaps.
Elements.Notes: For accessibility, avoid red-green color combinations. The 'viridis' and 'cividis' colormaps are perceptually uniform and colorblind-friendly alternatives.

Items.Text: Colors can be specified as named strings ('steelblue'), hex codes ('#2c7bb6'), or RGB tuples.
plt.cm.get_cmap() retrieves a colormap object for mapping continuous data to color.
The prop_cycle style parameter defines an automatic color sequence for multiple lines.

Elements.Title: Fonts, Tick Marks, and Spines
Elements.BodyText: Fine-tuning typography and axis decorations distinguishes a presentation-ready figure from a draft plot. Matplotlib allows control over font size, tick frequency, and axis border (spine) visibility.
Elements.Notes: Removing the top and right spines (ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)) creates a cleaner, more modern look commonly seen in scientific journals.

Items.Text: plt.rcParams['font.size'] sets a global default font size for all text elements.
ax.set_xticks() and ax.set_xticklabels() define custom tick positions and labels.
ax.tick_params() controls tick direction, length, width, and label font size.

Elements.Title: Saving High-Resolution Figures
Elements.BodyText: Exporting figures at sufficient resolution is critical for print publications and technical reports. The plt.savefig() function supports multiple formats and resolution settings.
Elements.Notes: Journals typically require figures at 300 DPI for raster formats. Vector formats (PDF, SVG) are resolution-independent and preferred when file size permits.

Items.Text: The dpi parameter in plt.savefig() controls dots per inch for raster output.
bbox_inches='tight' prevents axis labels from being clipped at the figure boundary.
Vector formats like PDF and SVG preserve sharpness at any zoom level and are ideal for technical documents.

Topics.Title: Introduction to Pandas: Series and DataFrames
Topics.Summary: Introduces the pandas library and its two primary data structures — Series and DataFrame — which form the foundation for data manipulation and analysis in Python.

Elements.Title: What is Pandas?
Elements.BodyText: Pandas is a Python library built on NumPy that provides high-performance, easy-to-use data structures for working with labeled and relational data. It is the standard tool for tabular data analysis in Python.
Elements.Notes: The name 'pandas' is derived from 'panel data,' an econometrics term for multidimensional structured data. It is conventionally imported as: import pandas as pd.

Items.Text: Pandas handles missing data gracefully, representing it as NaN and providing methods to fill or drop it.
It integrates directly with matplotlib, enabling one-line plotting of DataFrames and Series.
Pandas supports reading and writing many file formats including CSV, Excel, JSON, and SQL.

Elements.Title: The Pandas Series
Elements.BodyText: A Series is a one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding data of any type. It behaves like a NumPy array but with an associated index that labels each element.
Elements.Notes: Series can be thought of as a single column of a spreadsheet with a row index. The index defaults to integers but can be set to strings, dates, or other values.

Items.Text: A Series is created with pd.Series(data, index=labels) where data can be a list, dict, or array.
Elements are accessed by label using .loc[] or by integer position using .iloc[].
Arithmetic operations on Series are automatically aligned by index.

Elements.Title: The Pandas DataFrame
Elements.BodyText: A DataFrame is a two-dimensional labeled data structure with columns of potentially different types, analogous to a spreadsheet or SQL table. It is the primary pandas object for engineering datasets.
Elements.Notes: A DataFrame can be thought of as a dictionary of Series objects sharing the same index. This makes column access intuitive: df['column_name'] returns that column as a Series.

Items.Text: DataFrames are created with pd.DataFrame(data) where data can be a dict of lists, a 2D NumPy array, or another DataFrame.
df.shape returns a tuple of (rows, columns), useful for quickly assessing dataset size.
df.dtypes shows the data type of each column, which affects available operations.

Elements.Title: Inspecting a DataFrame
Elements.BodyText: Pandas provides several methods for quickly exploring the structure and content of a newly loaded dataset. These are essential first steps in any data analysis workflow.
Elements.Notes: Always inspect a dataset immediately after loading it to detect unexpected data types, missing values, or formatting issues before performing analysis.

Items.Text: df.head(n) and df.tail(n) display the first or last n rows of the DataFrame.
df.info() prints a concise summary of column names, non-null counts, and data types.
df.describe() generates summary statistics for all numeric columns.

Topics.Title: Reading, Filtering, and Sorting Data with Pandas
Topics.Summary: Demonstrates how to load datasets from files and then select, filter, and sort rows and columns to extract meaningful subsets for engineering analysis.

Elements.Title: Reading Data from Files
Elements.BodyText: Pandas provides read functions that import data from common file formats directly into a DataFrame. Reading CSV files is the most common operation in engineering data workflows.
Elements.Notes: pd.read_csv() includes many parameters for handling real-world data issues such as custom delimiters (sep), header rows (header), and encoding. For Excel files, use pd.read_excel().

Items.Text: pd.read_csv('filepath.csv') loads a comma-separated file into a DataFrame.
The index_col parameter designates a column as the row index during import.
na_values specifies additional strings that should be interpreted as missing values.

Elements.Title: Selecting Columns and Rows
Elements.BodyText: Pandas provides multiple methods for selecting subsets of a DataFrame by column name or row index. Mastery of these selection methods is fundamental to data manipulation.
Elements.Notes: Using .loc[] with row and column labels is the most readable and explicit selection method. .iloc[] is preferred when working with positional integer-based indexing.

Items.Text: df['col'] selects a single column as a Series; df[['col1', 'col2']] selects multiple columns as a DataFrame.
df.loc[row_label, col_label] selects data by label; df.iloc[row_int, col_int] selects by position.
Row slices are supported: df.loc[0:5] returns the first six rows by label.

Elements.Title: Filtering Rows with Boolean Conditions
Elements.BodyText: Boolean indexing allows rows to be selected based on conditions applied to one or more columns. This is the standard method for subsetting engineering data by threshold or category.
Elements.Notes: Multiple conditions are combined with & (and) and | (or) operators, and each condition must be wrapped in parentheses. The ~ operator negates a condition.

Items.Text: df[df['column'] > value] returns all rows where the column value exceeds a threshold.
Multiple conditions are combined with & or |: df[(df['A'] > 5) & (df['B'] == 'X')].
df.query('column > value') provides a string-based alternative for readable filtering.

Elements.Title: Sorting DataFrames
Elements.BodyText: Sorting a DataFrame rearranges rows based on the values in one or more columns. Sorted data is easier to inspect and is required as a preprocessing step for some analyses.
Elements.Notes: Sorting does not modify the original DataFrame unless inplace=True is set. By default, sort_values() returns a new sorted DataFrame.

Items.Text: df.sort_values('column') sorts the DataFrame in ascending order by the specified column.
ascending=False reverses the sort order to descending.
Sorting by multiple columns is supported by passing a list: df.sort_values(['col1', 'col2']).

Elements.Title: Adding and Modifying Columns
Elements.BodyText: New columns can be added to a DataFrame through direct assignment, enabling derived variables to be computed from existing data. This is essential for feature engineering and unit conversions.
Elements.Notes: Vectorized operations on columns are much faster than row-by-row Python loops and should always be preferred for performance-critical workflows.

Items.Text: df['new_col'] = df['col1'] + df['col2'] creates a new column from existing ones.
df['col'].apply(func) applies a custom Python function element-wise to a column.
df.rename(columns={'old': 'new'}) renames columns without modifying data.

Topics.Title: Descriptive Statistics with Pandas
Topics.Summary: Covers pandas methods for computing summary statistics, aggregating grouped data, and identifying missing values in engineering datasets.

Elements.Title: Summary Statistics with describe()
Elements.BodyText: The df.describe() method computes count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles for all numeric columns simultaneously. It provides a rapid overview of dataset characteristics.
Elements.Notes: Passing include='all' to df.describe() includes non-numeric columns, showing count, unique, top, and frequency statistics for categorical data.

Items.Text: df.describe() returns a DataFrame of statistics, making it easy to compare columns.
Individual statistics are also available as methods: df['col'].mean(), .std(), .min(), .max().
df['col'].value_counts() counts occurrences of each unique value in a categorical column.

Elements.Title: Aggregation with groupby()
Elements.BodyText: The groupby() method splits a DataFrame into groups based on column values and applies aggregate functions to each group. It is the pandas equivalent of SQL GROUP BY.
Elements.Notes: Chaining groupby with multiple aggregation functions is done with .agg(): df.groupby('category').agg({'col1': 'mean', 'col2': 'sum'}). This returns a multi-column summary table.

Items.Text: df.groupby('col').mean() computes the mean of all numeric columns for each group.
Multiple aggregation functions can be applied with .agg(['mean', 'std', 'count']).
Grouping by multiple columns is supported: df.groupby(['col1', 'col2']).sum().

Elements.Title: Handling Missing Values
Elements.BodyText: Missing values represented as NaN are common in real engineering datasets from sensor dropouts or incomplete records. Pandas provides methods to detect, remove, or fill missing data.
Elements.Notes: Blindly dropping all rows with missing values can significantly reduce dataset size. Imputation with mean or median values is a common alternative but should be applied carefully to avoid bias.

Items.Text: df.isnull().sum() counts the number of missing values in each column.
df.dropna() removes any row containing at least one NaN value.
df.fillna(value) replaces all NaN values with a specified constant or computed value.

Elements.Title: Correlation and Relationships
Elements.BodyText: Pandas can compute pairwise correlations between numeric columns, providing a quick measure of linear relationships within an engineering dataset.
Elements.Notes: Correlation values range from -1 to 1. A value near 1 indicates strong positive linear correlation; near -1 indicates strong negative linear correlation; near 0 indicates little to no linear relationship.

Items.Text: df.corr() returns a correlation matrix for all numeric columns.
df['col1'].corr(df['col2']) computes the correlation between two specific columns.
Correlation matrices are often visualized as heatmaps using matplotlib or the seaborn library.

Elements.Title: Plotting Directly from Pandas
Elements.BodyText: Pandas DataFrames and Series have built-in plot methods that call matplotlib internally, enabling quick exploratory visualizations with minimal code.
Elements.Notes: df.plot() accepts a kind parameter for different chart types: 'line', 'bar', 'barh', 'hist', 'scatter'. The resulting Axes object can be further customized with standard matplotlib commands.

Items.Text: df.plot(x='col1', y='col2', kind='scatter') creates a scatter plot directly from a DataFrame.
df['col'].plot(kind='hist', bins=20) generates a histogram of a single column.
The ax parameter passes an existing Axes object, allowing pandas plots to be embedded in subplot layouts.
